title: Unit 05 Pretest
description: Diagnostic pretest for Portfolio Development and Organization.

questions.prompt: What is the main purpose of a career portfolio?

choices.text: To replace every school assignment a student completes
choices.feedback: A portfolio may include selected work, but it does not replace every assignment.

choices.text: To list only personal hobbies with no explanation
choices.feedback: A career portfolio should include purposeful evidence connected to skills and goals.

choices.text: To avoid organizing work into categories
choices.feedback: Organization is an important part of making a portfolio useful and clear.

choices.text: To show evidence of skills, growth, and readiness
choices.feedback: Correct. A career portfolio is meant to show what a student can do and how prepared the student is for future opportunities.

questions.prompt: Which item is the best example of a portfolio artifact?

choices.text: A random blank sheet of paper
choices.feedback: A useful artifact should provide evidence of learning, skill, or achievement.

choices.text: A project, certificate, or work sample that shows ability
choices.feedback: Correct. Portfolio artifacts are pieces of evidence that demonstrate skills, growth, or accomplishments.

choices.text: An item with no connection to goals or strengths
choices.feedback: Artifacts should be chosen because they connect to important skills or achievements.

choices.text: A list of classes without any supporting evidence
choices.feedback: A class list alone does not provide strong evidence of skills or accomplishments.

questions.prompt: Organizing a portfolio into categories can make it easier for someone to review the student's work.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Categories help a reviewer find evidence quickly and understand the portfolio structure.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Categories improve clarity and make the portfolio easier to use.

questions.prompt: Which category would best help organize a portfolio?

choices.text: Favorite snacks
choices.feedback: Portfolio categories should relate to skills, achievements, or readiness.

choices.text: Unsorted Papers
choices.feedback: A portfolio should be organized, not built around being unsorted.

choices.text: Things I Forgot to Label
choices.feedback: Labels and clear organization are important parts of a strong portfolio.

choices.text: Skills and Certifications
choices.feedback: Correct. Categories like skills and certifications help present evidence clearly.

questions.prompt: What is one advantage of a digital portfolio?

choices.text: It cannot include images or links
choices.feedback: Digital portfolios can often include images, links, and other media.

choices.text: It is always better for every situation with no exceptions
choices.feedback: Digital portfolios have advantages, but the best format depends on the purpose and audience.

choices.text: It can be easy to update and share
choices.feedback: Correct. Digital portfolios are often easier to revise and share with others.

choices.text: It removes the need to organize materials
choices.feedback: Digital portfolios still need clear structure and organization.

questions.prompt: A physical portfolio can still be a useful choice in some situations.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Physical portfolios can still be useful, especially when printed samples or in-person review are needed.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. A physical portfolio may be helpful when a person wants to show printed materials directly.

questions.prompt: A student chooses a leadership certificate, a project summary, and a reflection about teamwork. What does this show about artifact selection?

choices.text: Artifacts should be selected to show different kinds of evidence
choices.feedback: Correct. Strong artifact selection shows a range of skills, experiences, and achievements.

choices.text: Artifacts should only come from one class
choices.feedback: Artifacts can come from different experiences if they support the portfolio purpose.

choices.text: Artifacts do not need to connect to strengths or readiness
choices.feedback: Artifacts should connect to what the student wants to show.

choices.text: Artifacts should never include reflection
choices.feedback: Reflection can strengthen a portfolio by explaining growth and choices.

questions.prompt: Why should a student think about audience when building a portfolio?

choices.text: Because the audience determines whether organization matters
choices.feedback: Organization matters for any audience.

choices.text: Because the student should include every document available
choices.feedback: A portfolio should be selective, not overloaded.

choices.text: Because audience and purpose can influence format and content choices
choices.feedback: Correct. Audience and purpose help guide what to include and how to present it.

choices.text: Because audience does not matter in a professional portfolio
choices.feedback: Audience matters because the portfolio should be clear and useful for the person reviewing it.
